item.title: Just a question to someone who made a research to contain a virus.
item.content:encoded: I wonder, how exactly can you 'capture' samples, like, detailed stages of collecting them, examinate them under microscopes and then freezing them in ice?

item.title: Viri
item.content:encoded:
Viruses can't be 100% lethal, then it doesn't work as a virus. It has to spread. If it kills the host, then that breaks the point of the virus, which is to proliferate. Alongside this, 100% lethality is physically impossible, give your immune system some credit, it's been around the block for literally all of human history, it's learned how to do its job.

Lethal viruses aren't lethal because they want to be or because it's an effective strategy, diseases that are lethal are lethal because they aren't made for humans. They're made for a different animal, a different biology, and so their attempts to hijack your system kill you. Every epidemic, plague, etc. all come from animals: HIV and AIDs(Monkeys, some say Chimps), Swine Flu(pigs), rabies(juries out chief), Avian flu(birbs), Antrax(Grazing animals, sheep, goats, cows, etc.), I could go on, but I'd just be spitting wiki after this.

Why do viruses guarantee immunity(not as you postulate, only the ones we know)? Well, because that how humans develop immunity.
It's not so much that's how viruses work as that's how your immune system works. Each of us builds millions of B Lymphocytes each of whom has hundreds of membrane-bound antibodies each perfectly tailored to an antigen. Viruses will always have some kind of hallmark and should a B Lymphocyte find one that matches, it'll mass copy and go Hunter-Killer on that virus. Oversimplifying and skipping some stuff but yeah.

item.title: Re: About the image
item.content:encoded:
I do apologize again, I didn't realize 12 years ago (holy %$&#) there would be consequences for not thoroughly identifying the original author of this SCP. I'm not even sure the image itself would have been able to be sourced, probably just something posted on a /x/ thread the original author used for inspiration.
It floors me how much the fanbase has grown since those days and I would have been a lot more careful had I known. Youthful ignorance and all that.

item.title: Re: About the image
item.content:encoded:
As indicated by the above comment by N_Aepic_Fael, the previous image for this SCP could not be sourced and was thus assumed non-compliant with the site's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, it was removed.

EDIT: Whoops, ninja'd by Zyn lol

item.title: Re: About the image
item.content:encoded:
If you scroll up a bit, the comment two posts above yours states:

The image used in this article could not be sourced, seeing as one of the earliest uses of this image was five years after this article was posted. As the original author is unknown, the compliance of this image could not be ascertained and it is assumed to be non-compliant with Creative Commons 3.0.

Basically it could not be determined if the SCP wiki could legally use the image, so it was removed.

item.title: About the image
item.content:encoded: Hey, just wondering, why was the image removed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how it explains a plethora of known zombie tropes with really convincing scientific jargon. That's a +1 from me.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The image used in this article could not be sourced, seeing as one of the earliest uses of this image was five years after this article was posted. As the original author is unknown, the compliance of this image could not be ascertained and it is assumed to be non-compliant with Creative Commons 3.0.

Edit: Image has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn’t surprising to me that one of the very first skips is a zombie virus. Still well written and surprisingly got clinical tone right before many other series 1 skip writers.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So looking at the effects of 008, something throws me off. It's says that it's "Not airborne or Waterborne". I can understand not being Waterborne, but how can it not be airborne? It's stated that there's strong evidence to shot that 008 may not have been formed on Earth, however it doesn't really show how the infection spreads into a host in the first place, and there are samples showing the virus's prion composition. But it doesn't show how the virus infects a host in the first place. And I can't find any detail as to how it does. So is this just another of those classified research things, or was there originally a way the host was infected, other than biting another uninfected host?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah that's true. It's unlikely, but I can hope. ^^; Though it would probably involve having to track down the original author, who's long gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is at +540, and the original author’s long gone. I don’t think it’ll ever get rewritten.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was re-reading this SCP, for old times sake. I've seen discussion about updating this SCP, to include more varied infectious effects, which is understandable, given how the zombie genre itself has been changed and influenced over the last eleven years. Infected hive minds, like in [PROTOTYPE], running zombies like in the Dawn of the Dead remake and 28 Days Later. Cordyceps fungi popularised in The Last of Us, the varied strains of infected in Left 4 Dead etc.

Anyway if this SCP did get updated, I would suggest the following changes.

  1. Images of infected. I am positive there are images of zombies that would fit with the site's image policies. Alternatively maybe an image contest could be held. This image would be placed further down the article, so as not to give away the reveal of a zombie virus, too soon.
  2. An explanation of the discovery of SCP-008, and maybe cooperation with groups besides the GRU, such as the GOC, UIU etc.
  3. Maybe have certain animals be vulnerable to infection? Animals that are commonly found near humans or close proximity, such as dogs, cats, horses, cows etc? But have the carrion creatures, vermin and insects instinctively ignore or refuse to eat SCP-008 tissue samples. It isn't uncommon for diseases to jump species after all. Unless SCP-008 has been tailored specifically to kill humans.
  4. An incident log, detailing an outbreak of SCP-008? Also it could be hinted that the real life plans to deal with zombie outbreaks by the US, UK and other governments was actually in response to SCP-008. Always fun when you can tie real-life to fiction like that. ^^

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: ben-m261, per the required reading, this is not a roleplay site. Please act accordingly in forum thread discussions.

item.title: Re: I hope I am not imposing...
item.content:encoded:
i agree as a spartan who has seen the flood Parasite first hand

So scp 008 is like the flood parasite

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: zombie virus hell ya its a great idea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
008, …zombies are great? Yeah, I got nothing.
Man, I remember the first time I saw this one. I had skipped(heh, skipped) the 001s, so when I reached this one and got hit by that warning, I thought it was so cool.
Now, it’s just a zombie virus. Sorry, prion.
Man, you know what we should do? Change it to thaumiel. The current object class just doesn’t do it for me.
Okay, but now seriously.
It’s a zombie vi- prion. That’s it. It’s not that interesting. The only thing this shows is that the foundation can contain diseases with ease. We already have better diseases than zombies. Just look at the clockwork virus. It fulfills the same basic premise, (turning people into mindless carriers of itself) but has a simple twist that makes it far more memorable and entertaining. This doesn’t. It just follows the dots.
-1.

item.title: Re: I hope I am not imposing...
item.content:encoded:
first of all, please dont hate me for necroing
isn't this just a zombie virus ported into the SCP universe?
writing standards have obviously improved since this article

item.title: Re: SCP-427 effect on SCP-008
item.content:encoded: Correct, SCP 427 is the locket created by putting SCP 500 through SCP 914 on Fine Setting. Cures a common cold in 2 minutes. Being in possesion of it for too long causes you to become a flesh blob thing.

item.title: Re: SCP-427 effect on SCP-008
item.content:encoded: Isn’t SCP-963 the Bright amulet? I’m pretty sure 427 is the flesh bob thing

item.title: Re: SCP-427 effect on SCP-008
item.content:encoded:
Even if that does cure it (which isn't certain), the person ''dies'' anyway because Bright takes them over. Not very useful. Leveritas is stupid and doesn't know anything.

Thanks for the correction Seagull 0.0

item.title: SCP-427 effect on SCP-008
item.content:encoded:
So, I have read credentials on SCP 008, SCP 500, SCP 427, & SCP 914. Here me out and then come to a conclusion because I have no idea what would happen.

SCP 500 is a pill that is known to cure SCP 008 even in advanced stages

SCP 427 is a locket created by the input of SCP 500 in SCP 914 on Fine.

So, the question of the day is: has anyone tried using SCP 427 to cure the effect on SCP 008?

item.title: Re: I created the page, but I'm not the original author.
item.content:encoded: Thanks for letting us know! We've changed the accreditation to Unknown Author to reflect this information.

item.title: Re: I created the page, but I'm not the original author.
item.content:encoded: Say no more my dude.

item.title: Re: I created the page, but I'm not the original author.
item.content:encoded:
Welcome back!

From my knowledge, the article will most likely stay here and the attribution metadata will change to not give you credit and say that the author is unknown

item.title: I created the page, but I'm not the original author.
item.content:encoded:
Hi all! I am Lee Byron, and I helped migrate the files from a previous server a long long time ago. As a result, I'm technically credited for writing this, but it's not actually my work. Unfortunately, I wasn't careful enough to source the work when it was migrated so I don't know who actually wrote it.
I leave this up to the community to decide what they wish to do with the file.

item.title: Re: Why is this an SCP?
item.content:encoded:
"Anomalous", in the case of the Foundation, doesn't mean impossible, but too far outside the normal.

For instance, SCP-1000 - not impossible at all, but extremely abnormal.

Similarly, a virus this horrifically lethal, and demonstratably unnatural, is far enough outside the normal to be within the purview of the Foundation. Sure, they could release the data, and it wouldn't our understanding of scientific facts too much - but does the Foundation really want people all across the world researching how to release a zombie virus?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, this is a pure demonstration of how a cliché anomaly (such as a zombie virus) can be easily contained in the SCPverse.

item.title: Why is this an SCP?
item.content:encoded: I could be wrong, but what is anomalous about this SCP? It seems extremely dangerous, but nothing about it seems to defy any natural laws. While such a pathogen seems fantastic, it could feasibly exist while conforming to all natural laws. Why is it so anomalous for a pathogen such as this to exist?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really raises the question, is the first iteration of a phenomenon really cliche?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One minute in: "Boy, do I love microbiology and brain prion disease!"
Five minutes in: "I wear a leather jacket, I have Lucille, and my nut sack is made of steel."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that just means the zombies can survive for over 10 years on its own without nutrition, considering it comes a number of steps after the coma onset.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So uhh

Coma onset approximately 20 hours after first symptoms appear
Coma onset will be considered onset of death.

vs

allowing subject to survive for over 10 years without nutrition.

which is it?

item.title: Re: Maybe make it more dangerous?
item.content:encoded:
To me, the point of this article isn't to show how dangerous a zombie plague would be; whether they would realistically end the world or not, you can't have zombies running (or shambling) around.

What makes this interesting is the focus on the procedures and ruthlessly pragmatic contingency plans (no site containing this is allowed to have an evacuation plan, etc.) the Foundation uses to keep it in check. It's as much a character piece about the SCPF itself as it is a contribution to the zombie genre.

item.title: Re: Maybe make it more dangerous?
item.content:encoded:
zombie apocalypse is more interesting

380 people who upvoted this article disagree with you.

Seriously, it looks like you went to this article right after playing Plague Inc.

item.title: Re: Maybe make it more dangerous?
item.content:encoded:
A zombie infection that's capable of realistically reaching "zombie apocalypse" levels is more interesting than one that can be easily contained even by untrained civilians.

I fundamentally disagree with the notion that more danger=more interesting.

item.title: Re: Maybe make it more dangerous?
item.content:encoded:
A zombie infection that's capable of realistically reaching "zombie apocalypse" levels is more interesting than one that can be easily contained even by untrained civilians. It's not the only issue with the entry, but seems to be what stands out the most at the moment.

Though, now that I'm looking at it again, I realize it is important to be careful with the changes to keep the approach in the article of trying to delay the realization by the reader that the entry is about zombies as further down the article as possible; that is one aspect the current entry has in its favor.

item.title: Re: Maybe make it more dangerous?
item.content:encoded: The point about this SCP, from a modern standpoint, is it doesn't do anything "new" regarding zombies, and has an overall lack of narrative (aside from the briefly-mentioned GRU-Foundation research cooperation and split). Simply making the SCP itself more dangerous won't solve those issues; while "more danger" might lead to bringing something new to the zombie side of things, you still have that critical lack of narrative to deal with, which is generally your best area to make a connection with the reader.

item.title: Re: Maybe make it more dangerous?
item.content:encoded:
in the same way that changing the color of a car won't really improve said car.

What are you talking about? Everyone knows red cars go faster!

But seriously now; having stuff in-character/plausible for the established universe, while an important factor, isn't enough; how much the contents impacts the reader makes a whole lot of difference. Taking a well known concept that's usually considered a challenge, and then offering a weakened version that's easily contained isn't all that appealing (at least if you don't compensate enough in other areas).

Regarding the "RL zombie downfalls" matter; pretty much everything can be worked around with a little creativity. You don't need it to be airborne to reach pandemic levels; having people not show significant signs of being sick but already being contagious plus air-travel can already spread it significantly; and if you really need it, have the infected people sneeze and/or cough, or have itchy and slightly teary eyes or something of the sort and have the contagion agent survive for a period on droplets/films of ejected bodily fluids. The weather situation can be dealt with by hibernation, changes in metabolism, production of anti-freeze chemicals etc. And the predation issue actually isn't an issue if other species can also be zombiefied or at least become carriers.

The matter of them not reproducing the usual way, is not gonna make much difference if the initial wave reached pandemic levels; sure, it won't be literally the end of the world, but the pockets of remaining humanity will have to survive without the support of society at large for a while, and once the zombies are gone (assuming a balance isn't reached between new infections and the birth rate of various species), they're gonna have to rebuild society almost from scratch, with seriously damaged ecosystems; and there is always the risk the contagion agent is still alive out there, ready to wipe the small tribes if they slip. Things might go slightly better if the Foundation survives and has the resources to more quickly fix things; but at the same time, things might get a lot worse if understaffing or exhaustion etc, directly or indirectly, causes containment breach of some the more serious skips.

item.title: Re: Maybe make it more dangerous?
item.content:encoded:
but would having an easily containable zombie plague really be better for the site than having something that is actually concerning?

Upping the danger alone tends to not be a good route for improving an article, in the same way that changing the color of a car won't really improve said car. What's more important- and the key to many articles- is how the Foundation and/or other organizations or people react to the SCP.

Besides, in real life, a Zombie virus would quickly be dealt with- the Zombie Apocalypse scenario could only ever work with a sudden global areosol event (unlikely as hell outside of human intervention), and even then would likely be a self-solving issue within a few months to a year, depending on the local environment and local predators/scavangers.

item.title: Maybe make it more dangerous?
item.content:encoded:
The way it's written, it sounds too tame. It would be a much more significant threat if it had a longer period before the undead-like symptoms manifest but with the infected already being capable of contagion, maybe counted in how many times the infected fall asleep (but with a more dangerous result if the infected stay awake for the amount of time that would kill an uninfected human; like maybe that would produce fast zombies instead of slow ones, possibly even super-strong, like they're on PCB or something, they use all the muscle fibers all the time because they don't feel pain or something like that); and perhaps during this period, the symptoms would be a gradual increase in sociability and a growing compulsion for physical contact, possibly even to the point of sexual promiscuity (perhaps even add a change in hormones/pheromones to make the infected appear more attractive), as well as a growing impulse to occasionally travel to new locations with significant population concentrations.

By having a period where the infected are not noticeably sick but already infecting others, and with an impulse to visit other population centers; it could much more easily reach a critical mass of zombies to cause a collapse of society before anyone realizes there is anything wrong.

Also, the "attracted to anything human-like" and "compulsion to eat human flesh" parts would need some tweaking to lead to hoards of zombies that don't just eat each-other; maybe they are attracted from a long distance, but once they're a few meters from another zombie or a pre-zombie infected, they can smell the changes and don't interpret each other as food? Besides fixing the self-consuming hoard issue, this also has the benefit of producing a herding behavior, ensuring that hoards don't easily dissipate due to the individuals moving randomly.

And I see no reason to make it incapacitate animals; if anything, it would be more interesting if symptoms on animals were similar to humans, but with the infection-focused behaviors less restricted to just the same species; perhaps even adding a tendency for swapping fear from predators with sexual attraction (somewhat similar to what happens with rats infected with toxoplasma gondii), during the pre-zombie stage.

I understand this is an old entry; but would having an easily containable zombie plague really be better for the site than having something that is actually concerning?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How is this cliché? It can't be cliché if it's the first of it's kind on here (and rather well done for it's time).

the level 4 clearence isn't needed.

Clearance*
Why would it not be needed?
That's like saying anybody can go to the CDC in real life and just view all of the information the government has on their samples of similar deadly, infectious diseases like Small Pox and Marburg, including how to keep them preserved and where they're located.

Makes no sense to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
no story to it

This is one of the oldest articles on the site, posted years before today's standards and expectations became common.

This is clichie,

*cliche. Or cliché, if someone's a stickler.

Also, for the record, "cliche" doesn't mean "don't ever do this again you'll fail". It just means something has been done enough times for it to feel common, and new attempts will need to approach things differently enough to be unique. Part of the reason cliches develop is because people like those things, and creators respond to that by making more of those things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is clichie, no story to it, and not to mention, the level 4 clearence isn't needed.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A zombie plague, no matter how well framed or mechanically competent, is still a zombie plague.

-1

item.title: Re: I hope I am not imposing...
item.content:encoded: It shouldn't, because in the foundation universe, it's easy to contain a zombie outbreak.

item.title: Re: I hope I am not imposing...
item.content:encoded: shouldn't it be keter

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: zombie plagues too over-used but I'm not stayin neutral on vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still annoyed about the fact that the containment procedures are classified. I think that the item number, object class, and SCP should be there with the description and addendum classified. However, the rest of the article is written well, so +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Closest equivalent is this:

Subject can adapt to its damaged nervous systems but is limited to basic physical activities, including standing up, balancing on two legs, walking, biting, grabbing, and crawling. Subject will energetically move towards sights, sounds, and smells it associates with living humans. Subject will attempt to ingest living humans if physical contact is made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am trying to find What is the designation for someone infected by SCP-008 or "SCP-008 infectee"? I've been looking, maybe I'm looking over it, but may someone help me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're not wrong, sadly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes exactly. I dont think the quality of this article justifies its privilege to pre-empt other potentially incredible articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is only popular for being old. The writing is below decent at best. It is -in my opinion- unneededly detailed and boring to read. It also could be explored more. I know that this is a placeholder, but it also closes the opportunity for many better SCP ideas. I highly recommend someone to do a rewrite, since while extremely cliche, I think the article has a lot of potential.
Definently an -1.

item.title: Isn't This Really, Really Cliche?
item.content:encoded: I honestly don't see any creativity in this besides the exploration logs. The "zombie virus" story is too old for this site. I know it's an old article, but I wish the author could have shown some creativity and made something new besides just explaining a zombie virus in excruciating detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a constant shift for object classes. We need a "dangerous as fuck if it gets out, possible K class scenario, but it usually doesn't and it's not that hard to contain" class.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
A recent edit was reverted, as it made a substantial content change without author or staff permission.

Given that this is not the first time an edit needed to be reverted, this page is now locked. Anyone wishing to make further changes should suggest them in the discussion thread.

item.title: illogical
item.content:encoded:
SCP-008 is transmittable only through physical contact with the someone infected or with the disease itself. Symptoms manifest in 3 hours, and no one is immune, meaning there are no asymptomatic carriers or incubation periods

So why is quarantine 4 months?!

For heaven's sake, a day would be enough. This is a waste of (fictional) resources.

Additionally, weird formatting aside, there's no actual need for such high security clearance requirements on this particular SCP, at lest any more so than the others. I also think it should remain Euclid-class, since really the biggest danger is a government synthesizing it, and that's far in the future.

In defense of SCP-008 in general, though, it's concise and thorough and it prevents dozens of similar SCPs from popping up. It may be unoriginal, but to me, the "boring" SCPs just make the other ones seen more interesting.
